target: data/sections.json part4 §4.2.2 — fix no-physical-mechanism misattribution + add Sq-residual fourth-horn (post-EXP-253 c2 follow-up)
replacement_html:
4.2.2 What the globe predicts

The dome’s eclipse argument depends on a specific claim about what standard physics predicts — and that claim is wrong. The dome’s published falsification criterion treats “globe predicts 0 nT” as the test; standard ionospheric physics (Chapman 1933) actually predicts 5–25 nT, confirmed across dozens of eclipses over nearly a century, and one of the dome’s own baseline inputs already sits outside its ±3 nT “globe” window.

The dome’s eclipse argument depends on a specific claim about what standard physics predicts — and that claim is wrong. From the dome’s live-power dashboard, Domain 11 (archived 2026-04-07; verified live at john09289.github.io/predictions/live.html on 2026-04-27):

Eclipse 2026 — Fails if measured anomaly is within ±3 nT of 0 (globe prediction).

And the dome’s eclipse countdown banner repeats the same framing more directly:

X days until Aug 12, 2026 eclipse — ECM predicts -[value] nT, globe predicts 0 nT. Requires Kp < 2.

The dome’s published falsification criterion and live countdown banner treat zero, not five-to-twenty-five, as the standard-physics expectation. This is the load-bearing error that lets the hedging structure (see §4.2.3) read like a discrimination test in the first place: any non-zero measurement at the prediction stations becomes “confirmation of ECM, falsification of globe” — but only because the globe’s actual prediction has been treated as zero in the dome’s published rule. What standard physics actually predicts is enough to overlap nearly all of the dome’s prediction window before any data is collected.

Eclipse-induced magnetic perturbations were first identified by Chapman (1933), who proposed that the Moon’s shadow reduces ionospheric UV ionization, lowering E-layer conductivity (at 90–150 km altitude) and disrupting the solar quiet (Sq) current system. This produces measurable ground-level magnetic perturbations that track the eclipse shadow geometry. Modern implementations of the Chapman mechanism — particularly the Ashour-Chapman models refined by Meza et al. (2021, arXiv:2107.12327) — match observed eclipse magnetic signals “strikingly accurately” at INTERMAGNET stations, predicting magnitudes of 5–25 nT depending on eclipse coverage, station latitude, local ionospheric conditions, and Sq current geometry. The literature is extensive: 207 observations across 39 eclipses (1991–2016) and a multi-station INTERMAGNET study of 4 total eclipses confirm the mechanism quantitatively.

The dome’s predictions.html does cite the Sq mechanism — the Tier 1 magnetic block reads “Globe prediction: 0.0 nT systematic (or ionospheric Sq disruption — competing mechanism exists, see WIN-068 honest caveat).” But the operational falsification rule, as published in the live-power dashboard and the eclipse countdown banner, still tests the measured anomaly against 0 nT, not against a pre-registered Sq-subtracted residual. Acknowledging Chapman’s Sq mechanism in a footnote and then writing the falsification rule as if Chapman doesn’t exist leaves the published test discriminating only against the strawman 0 nT, not against the 5–25 nT the Chapman mechanism — refined for over ninety years and matched to dozens of eclipse observations — actually predicts.

By the dome’s own published criterion, the dome has already failed. The dome’s eclipse formula uses a 2017 Boulder (BOU) observation of −10.9 nT as one of its baselines (see §4.2.5 for the full input chain). The BOU 2017 figure is itself a Chapman-mechanism measurement on a spherical Earth — the dome is, in effect, treating a globe-physics datum as input to a “globe predicts 0” prediction. By the dome’s own falsification rule (“within ±3 nT of 0”), −10.9 nT lies outside the window. The dome must either accept its own falsification criterion (and concede the “globe prediction” it asserts is wrong, since the BOU 2017 baseline is roughly 3.6× the falsification half-width (|−10.9| / 3 ≈ 3.6), well outside the dome’s own ±3 nT acceptance window) or revise the criterion (and concede the prediction was never discriminating in the first place).

A defender might try a third escape: that the ±3 nT rule applies to the post-Sq residual after Chapman has been subtracted out, not to the raw measurement, and that the WIN-068 honest_caveat is the disclosure of that residual reading. In that case, the dome owes a published, pre-registered Sq-subtraction methodology for the August 2026 measurement — one anchored to the data before it lands — so the test’s pass/fail outcome cannot be chosen after the fact. No such methodology is published anywhere on the dome’s site or in its OpenTimestamps record. Without it, “the rule means residual” is a moveable goalpost, not a defense; and on the only reading the dome has actually anchored — raw measured anomaly against 0 nT — the BOU-2017 trilemma above still fires.

(A second, independent failure appears in §4.2.7: the dome’s own published parameters produce a totality zone roughly 1.3 km wide — about 1/220 the observed path of totality — via an argument that does not depend on the misstatement established here.)

changes_made: H1 (MAJOR) — replaced the two-sentence 'no physical mechanism' attribution paragraph with a structural-critique paragraph that quotes the dome's actual Tier 1 disclosure ('Globe prediction: 0.0 nT systematic (or ionospheric Sq disruption — competing mechanism exists, see WIN-068 honest caveat)') and pivots to: dome cites Chapman in a footnote, then writes the operational falsification rule as if Chapman doesn't exist. Preserves the 'refined for over ninety years' beat by folding it into the new paragraph. Removes the misattributed 'no physical mechanism' phrase, which does not appear anywhere in the dome's predictions.html for the magnetic Tier 1 (only for gravity Tier 2/3, where the dome's published wording is 'cannot produce ground-level g changes' and 'no mechanism' respectively).

H2 (MODERATE) — added a new paragraph after the closing trilemma that addresses the post-Sq-residual fourth horn defense: dome owes pre-registered Sq-subtraction methodology before measurement; without it, residual reading is a moveable goalpost. Closes by reaffirming that the BOU-2017 trilemma still fires on the only reading the dome has actually anchored.

MINOR / polish — hoisted the §4.2.7 forward-link parenthetical to its own paragraph (was inline at end of trilemma paragraph). Implements c2 minor recommendation 4(a). Each closing argument now owns its own beat: trilemma paragraph → fourth-horn paragraph → §4.2.7 forward-link paragraph.
Updated 'verified live ... on 2026-04-26' to '2026-04-27' to match c2 drift_check capture date.
changes_NOT_made: TLDR third clause (c2 minor 'sits outside' phrasing) — left unchanged. The c2 marked it minor and optional; the body's closing trilemma rescues the framing for any reader who clicks past the TLDR. Editing the TLDR risks introducing a fresh micro-error and the body argument is now more robust against the third-escape, which is the readier line of defense than 'TLDR commits a category error'.

c1 stronger_arguments[0] (E001-E009 dome's own 2026 predictions all sit outside ±3 nT) — c2 marked this 'optional / not a hole / missed strengthening' and the trilemma without it is already tight; preserved scope discipline. If decider wants this added, it can be a separate ISS-followup (one sentence after the BOU-2017 hammer).

anticipated_objections.objection: The WIN-068 honest_caveat IS the pre-registered Sq-subtraction methodology — the dome has acknowledged the mechanism, so the rule is implicitly residual.
anticipated_objections.response: An acknowledgement is not a methodology. A pre-registered Sq-subtraction methodology has to specify (a) which Sq baseline is being subtracted, (b) what time-of-day window, (c) what station-set, and (d) what statistical aggregation — none of which appears in the dome's predictions.html, live.html, or any of its OTS-anchored files. WIN-068 says 'competing mechanism exists, see honest caveat' — that's a footnote disclosing the existence of an alternative, not an instruction for how to subtract it. The published operational rule still reads 'measured anomaly within ±3 nT of 0', and 0 is the strawman.

anticipated_objections.objection: By updating §4.2.2 we're effectively telling the dome author what to fix — is this gilding the lily or accelerating the responsiveness loop?
anticipated_objections.response: The hole was a real misattribution that gave a sophisticated dome defender a clean rebuttal ('we explicitly cite Sq, you're strawmanning us'). Leaving it unfixed weakens our review more than fixing it accelerates the dome's response. The fix also preserves the OTS canary structure: the dome's only path to a credible Sq-residual defense is to publish a pre-registered Sq-subtraction methodology in an OTS-anchored predictions document — which would, by construction, be the kind of separation-of-prediction-from-observation the canary is designed to detect. Either he fixes nothing (in which case our review stays sharper), or he fixes it the right way (in which case the canary fires).

anticipated_objections.objection: If the dome publishes a Sq-subtraction methodology after seeing this critique, doesn't that drain the trilemma?
anticipated_objections.response: It moves the line, but it doesn't drain it. Any post-hoc Sq-subtraction methodology that materializes only after our critique is published is, by definition, not pre-registered with respect to the observation — it's pre-registered with respect to the measurement only if anchored before August 12, 2026 in a separate OTS-anchored predictions document. If anchored that way, we update §4.2.2 to engage the published methodology on its merits (and the dome has correctly closed the goalpost-mobility hole, which is itself a positive structural move). If introduced retroactively without OTS anchoring, the moveable-goalpost critique fires harder than before. Either way, our position is robust.

kernel_of_truth: The dome's predictions.html does cite Chapman's Sq mechanism in the Tier 1 block — the WIN-068 honest_caveat is a real, non-trivial admission that a competing standard-physics mechanism exists for eclipse magnetic effects, and the dome author deserves credit for not pretending it doesn't. The misattribution we'd been carrying ('dome attributes no mechanism to globe') was unfair to that admission. The structural critique that survives the kernel-of-truth pass is sharper: the dome cites Chapman in a footnote, then writes the operational falsification rule as if Chapman doesn't exist. That's the load-bearing error, and it's still load-bearing once the strawman framing is removed.

newsworthy_note: Internal-quality fix to a section that already had its verdict integrated. No new claim or escalation — this is an EXP-253 follow-up patch tightening two corrigible holes the c2 review surfaced. Not a breaking-news beat.
user_preference_directive_capture: Per operator's user-preference directive ('Think about the other side of the argument too'): the strongest defender retreat from the patched §4.2.2 is now (a) 'we already published Sq in WIN-068' — addressed by the patched paragraph 5 distinction between footnote acknowledgement and operational rule; (b) 'rule applies to residual' — addressed by the new fourth-horn paragraph requiring pre-registered Sq-subtraction methodology; (c) 'we'll publish a Sq-subtraction methodology now' — addressed in anticipated_objections[2] by anchoring the pre-registration requirement to OTS+date-before-measurement, which structurally requires the canary fix. None of these defender retreats survive without a methodological commitment that, made properly, would itself constitute the canary-detected fix.
